county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cherokee County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Tahlequah, which served as the capital of the Cherokee Nation since 1839. Home to the Cherokee Heritage Center and Northeastern State University.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cherokee County is the heart of the Cherokee Nation, with the capital at Tahlequah. The Cherokee Heritage Center and Cherokee National Archives in Tahlequah hold the most comprehensive Cherokee genealogical records in existence: Cherokee census rolls (1817-1924), Dawes Roll cards and applications, Old Settler Rolls, Emigrant Rolls (Trail of Tears 1838-1839), Drennen Roll (1851), Cherokee Freedmen rolls, allotment records, and tribal court proceedings. The Cherokee Nation Registration Department provides genealogical assistance. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs records for the Union Agency (Muskogee). Northeastern State University in Tahlequah has regional history collections. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records (US Court, Northern District). County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). For Civil War research, Cherokee soldiers served both sides -- Confederate Cherokee records include Stand Watie's brigade.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Cherokee County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Choctaw County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Hugo. Named for the Choctaw people. Located in far southeastern Oklahoma along the Red River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Choctaw County was part of the Choctaw Nation in Indian Territory. Pre-statehood records center on Choctaw tribal government records: Dawes Rolls (Choctaw by Blood, Freedmen, Mississippi Choctaw), allotment records, and Choctaw Nation court records. NARA Fort Worth holds Choctaw Agency records, annuity rolls, and tribal census records. The Choctaw Nation headquarters in Durant maintains enrollment and historical files. County Clerk records in Hugo begin at statehood (1907). The area has significant African American genealogical records through Choctaw Freedmen rolls. For Civil War era, the Choctaw Nation allied with the Confederacy -- look for Confederate service records at NARA. The Hugo Heritage Railroad Museum and Choctaw County Historical Society have local materials. FamilySearch has indexed Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Choctaw County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.parent_county: Beaver County, Oklahoma Territory (No Man's Land)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cimarron County formed 1907 at statehood from Beaver County. County seat is Boise City. Westernmost county in the Oklahoma Panhandle. Named for the Cimarron River. Severely impacted by the Dust Bowl. Black Mesa, Oklahoma's highest point, is in this county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cimarron County is in the westernmost Oklahoma Panhandle, formerly 'No Man's Land.' It was created from Beaver County in 1907. Settlement was sparse until the 1880s-1890s. The county was devastated by the Dust Bowl in the 1930s, losing much of its population. County Clerk records in Boise City begin from 1907, with earlier records under Beaver County. BLM GLO land patents are available online. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial-era newspapers and census records. The Cimarron Heritage Center in Boise City has local historical materials including pioneer family records. Ranching records (brand registrations, livestock associations) are important for family research. FamilySearch has Oklahoma county marriage records. The Boise City bombing (1943) -- the only continental US city bombed during WWII (accidentally by a US Army training plane) -- is locally documented.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (1889 Land Run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cleveland County formed 1890 as original Oklahoma Territory county from the 1889 Land Run. County seat is Norman, home to the University of Oklahoma. Named for Grover Cleveland. One of the most populous Oklahoma counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cleveland County was one of the original Oklahoma Territory counties formed after the 1889 Land Run. Norman, the county seat, is home to the University of Oklahoma. County Clerk records begin from 1889, among the oldest in Oklahoma. BLM GLO land patents document original claims from the Land Run. The University of Oklahoma Western History Collections hold extensive manuscript materials, oral histories, and photograph collections for Oklahoma history. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and court records. Cleveland County has some of the most complete territorial-era county records in Oklahoma. FamilySearch has territorial marriage records. The Cleveland County Historical Society and Pioneer Library System have local genealogical collections. The Moore Public Library has Norman Transcript newspaper back files.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Coal County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory (Tobucksy County). County seat is Coalgate. Named for the coal mining industry. Italian, Welsh, and Eastern European immigrant mining communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Coal County was part of the Choctaw Nation (Tobucksy County) in Indian Territory. Named for its coal mining industry, the county had significant immigrant populations drawn to the mines -- Italian, Welsh, and Eastern European miners. Pre-statehood records include Choctaw Nation tribal records at NARA Fort Worth and the Choctaw Nation headquarters. Coal mining company records (employment, payroll, accident reports) may survive at the Oklahoma Historical Society or in private collections. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). Dawes Roll records (Choctaw by Blood, Choctaw Freedmen) are critical for tribal families. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records. Cemetery transcriptions have been published for many Coal County mining-community cemeteries. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Coal County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Comanche County formed 1901 after the KCA lands lottery. County seat is Lawton, founded the day of the lottery. Home to Fort Sill (est. 1869). Named for the Comanche people. Major military installation presence.
county_metadata.research_tips: Comanche County was organized in 1901 after the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache lands were opened by lottery. Lawton, the county seat, was founded on the day of the lottery. Fort Sill (established 1869) is a major military installation in the county. NARA Fort Worth holds extensive Kiowa, Comanche, and Apache Agency records (allotments, annuity rolls, agency correspondence). Fort Sill Military Reservation records (post records, post cemetery, post hospital) at NARA are genealogically valuable. The Fort Sill National Historic Landmark and Museum hold military and tribal history materials. County Clerk records begin from 1901. BLM GLO land patents document settler claims. The Museum of the Great Plains in Lawton has local history collections. FamilySearch has Oklahoma county marriage and probate records. Geronimo and other Apache prisoners of war were held at Fort Sill (1894-1913).

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Cotton County formed 1912 from Comanche County. County seat is Walters. One of the youngest Oklahoma counties. Named for the cotton crop. Located in southwestern Oklahoma.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cotton County was created in 1912 from Comanche County, making it one of the youngest counties in Oklahoma. For records before 1912, check Comanche County. County Clerk records in Walters begin from 1912. The county was part of the Kiowa-Comanche-Apache lottery area (1901). NARA Fort Worth holds tribal allotment records for the area. BLM GLO land patents document original settler claims. Cotton County was primarily agricultural (cotton, as the name suggests). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds newspapers and territorial records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma county marriage records. Cemetery transcriptions are available through Find A Grave and local volunteers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Craig County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Vinita, one of the first incorporated towns in Indian Territory (1898). Named for Granville Craig.
county_metadata.research_tips: Craig County was part of the Cherokee Nation in Indian Territory. Vinita, the county seat, was one of the first incorporated towns in Indian Territory (1898). The county has mixed Cherokee Nation and smaller tribal (Delaware/Lenape, Shawnee, Peoria) heritage. Cherokee Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls, and allotment records are essential. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs Union Agency records (Muskogee). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records (Northern District, Vinita sessions). County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Eastern Trails Museum in Vinita has local history materials. The Vinita Daily Journal newspaper archives may be available through the Oklahoma Historical Society. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Craig County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Creek County formed 1907 at statehood from the Creek (Muscogee) Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Sapulpa. Major oil boom area. Home to several All-Black towns. Named for the Creek people.
county_metadata.research_tips: Creek County was part of the Creek (Muscogee) Nation in Indian Territory. The county experienced a massive oil boom in the early 1900s, particularly around Bristow, Drumright, and Shamrock. Creek (Muscogee) Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls (Creek by Blood, Creek Freedmen), and allotment records are critical. NARA Fort Worth holds Creek Agency records and Bureau of Indian Affairs Union Agency (Muskogee) files. Oil lease records, royalty documents, and guardianship records (especially for minor allottees) are at the County Clerk and District Court. Creek County had several All-Black towns including Boley (one of the largest). County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. Creek County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Creek County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.source_name: Creek County Deed Books / Creek (Muscogee) Nation Allotment Records -- County Clerk / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Creek County Clerk, Sapulpa / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County deed books for Creek County from 1907 (statehood); pre-statehood land was held as Creek (Muscogee) Nation allotments under the Dawes Act -- allotment records at NARA Fort Worth and OHS; County Clerk maintains deeds, mortgages, and liens
source_profiles.survival_notes: County records complete from 1907; allotment records at NARA are substantially complete

source_profiles.source_name: Creek County Probate Records -- District Court
source_profiles.repository: Creek County District Court, Sapulpa
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements for Creek County; District Court has jurisdiction over probate in Oklahoma; guardianship records for minor allottees are particularly important in former Indian Territory counties

source_profiles.source_name: Dawes Rolls -- Creek (Muscogee) Nation Enrollment Records / NARA
source_profiles.repository: NARA Fort Worth / Creek (Muscogee) Nation / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Dawes Commission enrollment records for Creek (Muscogee) citizens (Creek (Muscogee) by Blood, Creek (Muscogee) Freedmen) in Creek County area; includes application jackets with family information, census cards, and land allotment details; essential for tracing Creek (Muscogee) families before and after statehood

source_profiles.source_name: Creek County Federal and Tribal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1900-1950 for Creek County; Creek (Muscogee) Nation census records and Indian Territory schedules at OHS and NARA

source_profiles.source_name: Creek County Oil Lease and Corporation Records -- County Clerk / Corporation Commission
source_profiles.repository: Creek County Clerk, Sapulpa / Oklahoma Corporation Commission, Oklahoma City
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Oil and gas lease records, royalty agreements, well permits, and corporation filings for Creek County; County Clerk records oil/gas leases; Oklahoma Corporation Commission in Oklahoma City holds well records and production data; guardianship records for minor allottees with oil wealth are at the District Court

source_profiles.source_name: Creek County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / OHS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Creek County Clerk, Sapulpa / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records for Creek County from 1907; County Clerk issued marriage licenses in Oklahoma; OHS holds some county marriage records on microfilm; FamilySearch has indexed Oklahoma marriages; state vital records registration began 1908

source_profiles.source_name: Creek County Indian Territory Court Records -- OHS / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Oklahoma Historical Society / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indian Territory court records for the Creek County area; US courts for Indian Territory (Northern, Central, Southern districts) handled criminal and civil cases; OHS holds court records, case files, and dockets; NARA Fort Worth has federal court records

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Custer County formed 1892 from former Cheyenne and Arapaho lands. County seat is Arapaho. Named for George Armstrong Custer. Located in western Oklahoma.
county_metadata.research_tips: Custer County was organized from former Cheyenne and Arapaho lands opened to settlement in 1892. County Clerk records in Arapaho begin from 1892. The town of Arapaho reflects the county's tribal heritage. NARA Fort Worth holds Cheyenne-Arapaho Agency records including allotment files and annuity rolls. BLM GLO land patents are available online for original settler claims. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and court records. Southwestern Oklahoma State University in Weatherford (in adjacent county) has regional history collections. The Custer County Historical Society has local records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial and state marriage records. Route 66 passed through the county, and early highway-era records may be locally available.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Custer County; County Clerk deed books from 1892

source_profiles.source_name: Custer County Deed Books / BLM GLO Patents -- County Clerk
source_profiles.repository: Custer County Clerk, Arapaho / Bureau of Land Management
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County deed books for Custer County from 1892; BLM GLO federal land patents document original claims from the territorial opening; County Clerk maintains deeds, mortgages, and liens
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1892; GLO patents are complete for federal land entries

source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / OKGenWeb / Custer County Historical Society
